skill.name: redis-patterns
skill.description: Provides practical Upstash Redis examples for caching, rate limiting, sessions, pub/sub, leaderboards, and reusable cache-aside helpers.
skill.summary: Apply common Upstash Redis patterns in TypeScript services.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: The external command findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, not Ruby or shell execution. The skill does show legitimate process.env access for Upstash Redis credentials, but I found no data exfiltration, prompt injection, command execution, or malicious intent in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access for Redis Credentials
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: SKILL.md reads UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_URL and UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_TOKEN from process.env. This is a normal configuration pattern for Redis clients, but users should treat these values as secrets and avoid logging or exposing them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly references process.env for Upstash Redis configuration. The context is client setup and does not show network exfiltration or misuse of the secret values.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are TypeScript examples inside Markdown code fences. I found no executable script file, shell command, Ruby interpolation, or user-controlled command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every external command hit aligns with Markdown triple-backtick code blocks. The reviewed content contains instructional TypeScript snippets, not executable backtick command substitutions.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive File and Weak Crypto Findings Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The sensitive environment file findings at lines 15 and 16 refer to environment variables, not a .env file read. The weak cryptographic algorithm finding at line 3 has no supporting evidence in the description text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a plain skill description, and lines 15 to 16 only reference process.env keys. I did not find file reads, cryptographic algorithm use, or malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Redis Secret Configuration via Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The Redis client example depends on process.env values for the Upstash REST URL and token. This is acceptable for configuration, but it is still a secret-handling pattern that should be reviewed before use in production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret references are explicit and limited to Redis client initialization. There is no evidence that the skill prints, transmits, or stores the token elsewhere.

content.user_title: Add Redis Caching and Rate Limits
content.value_statement: Teams often repeat Redis setup and cache logic across services. This skill gives concise Upstash Redis patterns that Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can adapt for TypeScript projects.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to initialize an Upstash Redis client with environment variables.
Demonstrates cache-aside reads with TTL-based Redis storage.
Provides cache invalidation examples for updated user records and list caches.
Includes Upstash rate limiting with response headers for API routes.
Covers Redis-backed sessions, pub/sub publishing, and sorted-set leaderboards.
limitations: It contains examples only and does not include runnable project scaffolding.
It assumes a TypeScript application with Upstash packages already installed.
It does not cover Redis cluster operations or self-hosted Redis administration.
It does not include automated tests, benchmarks, or production monitoring guidance.

use_cases.title: Add API Rate Limits
use_cases.description: Use the rate limiting pattern to protect API routes from repeated requests while returning standard limit headers.

use_cases.title: Speed Up Database Reads
use_cases.description: Use the cache-aside examples to add Redis TTL caching around common user or data lookups.

use_cases.title: Prototype Real-Time Features
use_cases.description: Use pub/sub, sessions, and leaderboard snippets as starting points for interactive application features.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Redis Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing TypeScript project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the redis-patterns skill to add an Upstash Redis client to my TypeScript app. Show the required environment variables and the smallest safe client file.

prompt_templates.title: Cache a Database Lookup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the redis-patterns skill to wrap this database lookup with Redis cache-aside logic. Include a TTL, a stable cache key, and clear invalidation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Protect an API Route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the redis-patterns skill to add Upstash rate limiting to this API route. Return proper 429 responses and standard rate limit headers.

prompt_templates.title: Design Redis Usage for a Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the redis-patterns skill to design Redis caching, session storage, pub/sub, and leaderboard usage for this feature. Explain key names, TTL choices, and invalidation risks.

output_examples.input: Ask for a Redis cache around a user lookup.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a cache key format, a five-minute TTL, a cache-first read path, and invalidation after user updates.

output_examples.input: Ask for API rate limiting with Upstash.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes a sliding-window limit, IP-based identifiers, 429 responses, and headers for limit, remaining requests, and reset time.

output_examples.input: Ask how to store sessions in Redis.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines random session IDs, Redis TTL storage, session lookup, and deletion during sign-out.

best_practices: Use stable key prefixes that identify the entity, scope, and version of cached data.
Set explicit TTL values for cached data and sessions to limit stale or abandoned records.
Invalidate related list and detail keys after writes that change user-visible data.
anti_patterns: Do not store Upstash tokens in source files or client-side bundles.
Do not cache sensitive user data without reviewing access controls and retention needs.
Do not use request IP alone for high-value abuse protection without proxy and identity checks.

faq.question: Does this skill install Redis packages?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and assumes the needed Upstash packages are installed separately.

faq.question: Can I use these examples with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for adapting Redis patterns.

faq.question: Does it support self-hosted Redis?
faq.answer: The examples target Upstash Redis. Some ideas transfer, but client setup and deployment details will differ.

faq.question: Are the cache examples production ready?
faq.answer: They are starting points. Review TTLs, key naming, error handling, privacy, and observability before production use.

faq.question: Does this skill handle cache invalidation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for deleting detail and list cache keys after updates.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It shows environment-based secret loading. You should still protect tokens and avoid exposing Redis credentials.
